items.question: Is polynomial regression linear or non-linear?
items.answer: It is linear in the coefficients (parameters) but non-linear in the raw input x because of terms like x^2. Optimization uses linear regression on expanded features, not general non-linear least squares.

items.question: How many parameters does a degree-d polynomial in one feature have (with intercept)?
items.answer: d + 1 parameters: b0 through bd for terms 1, x, x^2, ..., x^d.

items.question: Why can high-degree polynomials overfit even with linear regression?
items.answer: Each added power is an extra parameter that can absorb noise. With enough parameters relative to sample size, the model fits training idiosyncrasies, lowering training error while validation error rises.

items.question: You have one feature and 30 noisy samples along a smooth curve. How do you pick degree?
items.answer: Build a pipeline with scaling, PolynomialFeatures for degrees 1–5, and ridge or linear regression; run cross-validation; choose the lowest degree whose mean CV error is within one standard error of the best, avoiding high-degree wiggles.
